title: Needle felting onto fabric
subtitle: Working merino roving into a woven or knitted background to create surface motifs
excerpt: Needle felting onto an existing woven or knitted fabric locks merino roving fibres directly into the weave, creating applied motifs, colour fills, or textured patches without seams. The method is useful for decorating wool jumpers, adding botanical motifs to felt panels, or repairing moth damage. The background fabric must contain wool or a significant natural-fibre component for the fibres to lock.
sourceNotes: Needle felting applied to fabric backgrounds is a standard repair and decoration technique in contemporary textile practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The entanglement of applied roving fibres with the fibres of the background fabric. The barbed felting needle pushes roving fibres through the weave and under the surface, locking them physically to the woven threads.
The boundary shape of a needle-felted motif on a fabric background, worked first with a fine needle to define the edge before filling with colour.

content.text: : barbed needles push applied roving fibres through the weave of the background fabric so they entangle with the fabric's own fibres and cannot be pulled out. The background must contain at least 50% wool or a similar natural fibre (alpaca, cashmere) for the lock to hold. Synthetic backgrounds do not accept needle-felted fibres.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.

content.text: Step 1: Place the fabric on the mat

Lay the background fabric face-up on the foam mat. The foam must be underneath to receive the needle tip on each stroke. If the fabric is thick (a woven wool coat weight), place it directly on the mat. If it is thin (a knitted jersey), place a layer of spare roving between fabric and mat to prevent the mat fibres from locking into the back of the work.
Place a very thin strand of roving along the

on the fabric surface. Needle it down with a size 40 fine needle, working from the centre of the line outward and then back, keeping the strand in place without spreading it. The outline will compact to a fine raised line.

Lay thin wisps of roving within the outlined shape. Use a size 38 needle and work in a consistent direction across the fill area. The fill will sink slightly below the outline; this is normal. Build up thin layers rather than pressing one thick layer in all at once. Two or three light passes produce a smoother surface than one heavy pass.
Step 4: Define the edge and check coverage

Use the fine needle to work around the motif edge once more, pressing any stray fibres inward. Hold the piece up to the light to check for thin patches. Add more roving to thin areas and re-needle. The motif is complete when it resists a gentle nail-scratch test: fibres should not lift.

items.problem: The applied roving pulls off with handling
items.cause: The background fabric has a high synthetic content and the fibres cannot lock
items.fix: Test a small area first. If the roving pulls off cleanly after needling, the background is not suitable. Switch to a wool-rich fabric.

items.problem: The motif edges are fuzzy and undefined
items.cause: Roving applied too thickly at the edge, or outline not worked first
items.fix: Always outline first with a fine needle, then fill. Keep the fill layers thin and contain any stray fibres back within the outline.

items.problem: The background fabric shows holes or damage
items.cause: Too many needle strokes in the same spot, or needle used at an angle that tore the weave
items.fix: Work vertical strokes only and distribute strokes evenly. Reduce needle gauge if the background is delicate.
